title: Exposure to grass pollen--but not birch pollen--affects lung function in Swedish children.
ehr_evidence: The study uses the Swedish birth cohort BAMSE, which involves clinical examinations and blood sampling, but does not mention the use of electronic health records (EHR).
summary: A study investigating the impact of nine types of pollen and air pollution on lung function in Swedish children aged 8 and 16.
key_findings: Exposure to grass pollen the day before testing was associated with reduced FEV1 in 8-year-olds.
The negative effect of grass pollen was stronger in children sensitized to pollen allergens.
Birch pollen did not show a significant effect on lung function.
population: Swedish children (8 and 16 years old) in the Stockholm region
limitations: Study limited to the Stockholm region
